video_title: Want less sprawl and more urban infill? Try a land value tax!
gist: David Roberts interviews Greg Miller (Center for Land Economics) and Spokane city councilmember Kitty Klitsky on shifting property tax from buildings to land (land value / split-rate tax) to penalize speculation and vacant lots and reward urban infill. They cover the Georgist rationale, real-world cases (Pennsylvania, Vancouver, Estonia, Singapore), Washington's constitutional workaround via a universal building exemption, Spokane's modeling, and surprisingly bipartisan legislative momentum.

insights.claim: Standard property taxes bundle two separate taxes — a tax on land value and a tax on building value — under one rate, which means improving a lot (constructing or upgrading a building) mechanically raises the owner's tax bill and thereby penalizes development while rewarding leaving land idle.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Frames why zoning reform alone fails to unlock infill; investors in urban land should watch tax-code reform as a separate lever on development economics.
insights.quote: property taxes as currently constructed penalize building and reward leaving land underdeveloped

insights.claim: Land value is created not by the owner but by the surrounding community and public investment (roads, schools, transit, agglomeration), so a land value tax is framed as 'land value return' — recapturing publicly created value rather than taxing private effort.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: The moral/economic framing is what lets the policy attract cross-ideological support; useful for gauging political durability.
insights.quote: land is people sometimes get confused what we what do we mean by land, right? What we really mean is location and nobody creates location. It's a natural resource.

insights.claim: US land is estimated at roughly $50 trillion, described as the country's largest natural resource, none of which was created by its owners — the base that a land value tax targets.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Sizes the untaxed/underutilized asset class land-value reformers are aiming at.
insights.quote: we have $50 trillion of land in the United States. That's our largest natural resource.

insights.claim: Because land supply is fixed, a tax on land cannot reduce the quantity of land the way income/sales/carbon taxes reduce their bases — the classic economist argument for land taxation, endorsed even by Milton Friedman who called it 'the least bad tax'.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: The zero-deadweight-loss property is why conservative economists back it; signals the policy is efficiency-enhancing, not just redistributive.
insights.quote: you can't get less land, right? You can tax land all you want. You can't reduce the supply of land.

insights.claim: Modern land value tax advocates are not proposing George's single-tax vision; they explicitly propose a revenue-neutral shift that lowers the building tax and raises the land tax while keeping total city revenue unchanged, treating how revenue is collected — not how much — as the lever on urban form.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Revenue-neutral framing lowers political risk and reframes the debate from 'tax hike' to 'tax mix', changing who wins/loses.
insights.quote: It's not a question of how much money the government takes in... It's a question of how we collect that money

insights.claim: Disincentivizing building in urban cores is 'killing the golden goose' because agglomeration is what created the land's value in the first place, making current tax structures long-term self-destructive for city land values.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Argues underdevelopment erodes the very rents landowners are hoarding for — a bear case on long-run speculative land holds.
insights.quote: you're killing the golden goose that gave your land the value in the first place. It's it's long-term self-destructive.

insights.claim: Under current property tax systems a downtown vacant lot or surface parking lot typically pays 10x to 20x less in tax than the apartment complex next door; a shift to land value taxation would move those bills toward parity, raising the vacant lot's bill.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Quantifies the current subsidy to idle land; a repricing of that gap directly hits the carrying-cost math of land bankers.
insights.quote: the vacant lot pays today under the current property tax system, it's paying usually 10 times to 20 times less than the apartment complex sitting right next door

insights.claim: The effect of a land value tax is felt most intensely on the most valuable land, which is mechanically closer to the urban core, so the natural consequence is to push more building toward the core — the infill outcome reformers want.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Predicts geographic concentration of development pressure; core-adjacent parcels are where the policy bites hardest.

insights.claim: A 1% building tax is far larger than it appears because it applies every year for the life of the structure; amortized to net present value the building tax burden can equate to a roughly 20-30% one-time tax on the investment, making it a major disincentive to develop.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Reframes a nominally small annual tax as a large NPV drag on development pro formas — the core of the pro-build argument.
insights.quote: We take the net present value of that... it's it's can go up to 20 30% tax burden.

insights.claim: Taxing building value disincentivizes quality, durable materials (stone, brick), and maintenance, pushing developers toward cheap, short-lived construction because higher-value buildings incur higher taxes.
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: Suggests tax mix, not just developer greed, shapes construction quality — a second-order effect relevant to real-estate durability.
insights.quote: when we tax the value of a building we disincentivize quality. We disincentivize good materials and we disincentivize maintenance.

insights.claim: Peru illustrates the perverse-incentive point in reverse: buildings left incomplete (rebar sticking out of top floors) are taxed less than finished ones, so owners deliberately never complete structures across cities from Lima to Cusco.
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: Real-world evidence that taxing improvements distorts building behavior — supports the case against building taxes.
insights.quote: They don't tax buildings that are incomplete as highly as complete buildings. And so people just don't complete the building.

insights.claim: Land value taxes and YIMBY upzoning are complements, not substitutes: upzoning and transit-oriented development raise land values (creating a private windfall) but do not compel building, so a land tax is needed to capture that windfall and pressure owners to develop rather than sit.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Implies markets that only upzone (without tax reform) will keep seeing land-banking; the pairing is where infill actually materializes.
insights.quote: when we upzone, when we make it easier to develop, land becomes more valuable... you see all of that land become a lot more valuable. And because it becomes more valuable, folks can still sit on that land.

insights.claim: In the DC area (the Wharf-area developments), owners have sat on vacant upzoned land because they can wait 20 years and still double their money, illustrating how upzoning without a land tax rewards speculation.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Concrete case that low carrying costs make multi-decade land holds rational; the behavior a land tax aims to break.
insights.quote: they can wait 20 years and still double their money on that vacant plot of land

insights.claim: For many mid-sized cities the binding constraint on infill is incentives, not zoning: downtown surface parking lots that are already legally developable stay undeveloped, so upzoning alone (as done in Spokane) fails to trigger redevelopment.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Signals that zoning-only reform plays (and the thesis that upzoning unlocks supply) may underdeliver outside superstar cities.
insights.quote: we have this surface parking lot downtown that technically could be developed into something, but it's not. So, is zoning really our problem?

insights.claim: Over 20 Pennsylvania cities implemented split-rate taxes; Pittsburgh (the largest, in place ~1960-2000) weathered post-industrial decline better than neighbors, and economic studies associate the system with more housing infill, more units per acre, more permitting, less vacancy, and higher overall property values.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: The strongest empirical evidence base for LVT outcomes; caveated by 1980s-2000s data vintage.
insights.quote: you saw an increase in housing infill development, more units per acre, you saw more permitting, you saw less vacancy, less vacant land, and you saw an overall increase in property values

insights.claim: Pittsburgh repealed its split-rate tax in 2000 not because the tax failed but because a long-delayed reassessment (Pennsylvania has some assessments dating to the 1960s and does not mandate reassessment) produced sudden large valuation jumps and a populist backlash that scapegoated the split-rate system.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Key implementation lesson: infrequent reassessment is the political landmine, not the tax structure itself.
insights.quote: Pennsylvania also has the worst some of the worst reassessment policies

insights.claim: Syracuse, New York recently did a reassessment and voters rejected the reassessed values because of the resulting tax-burden increase — a live example of reassessment backlash that mirrors Pittsburgh's.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Confirms reassessment backlash is a recurring, current political risk for any property-tax reform.
insights.quote: We're seeing this today in Syracuse, New York. they did a reassessment and they voted against their reassessed values

insights.claim: Vancouver BC ran a nearly fully Georgist (land-only) tax in the early 20th century that was gradually whittled away as the building tax grew, disappearing entirely around the 1980s — after which the city developed a severe housing affordability crisis driven by lack of building.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Cautionary case: land taxes erode over time under homeowner pressure, and their loss coincides with affordability breakdown.
insights.quote: the building tax grew over time and then eventually, I think it was in the 80s. It finally went away entirely

insights.claim: Land value taxes are politically fragile because those penalized (homeowners/landowners) are a concentrated, powerful voting bloc while beneficiaries (future renters, the diffuse public) are unorganized and often unaware they benefit — the dynamic that killed Vancouver's system.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Explains the structural political headwind; a durable reform must neutralize homeowner opposition (e.g., by cutting their bills).
insights.quote: homeowners... are also an incredibly powerful political constituency. And the people who benefit from this policy... are sort of diffuse

insights.claim: Florida's push to abolish property taxes ended as merely a $250,000 homestead exemption for homeowners, illustrating how landowner/homeowner political power captures tax reform; a Charlie Kirk show host framed it as a wealth transfer from Gen Z to boomers.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Shows the default direction of property-tax politics favors incumbent owners over renters and new entrants.
insights.quote: there's a huge push to abolish property taxes that ended up just being a $250,000 exemption for homeowners

insights.claim: International precedents for capturing publicly created land value include Estonia's national land value tax (which just completed a nationwide reassessment), a German state that recently implemented one, and Singapore, which owns ~80% of its land and uses variable-rate leases that function as a land value tax underpinning its public finances.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the mechanism works at national scale via multiple designs (tax vs. public land ownership + leasing).
insights.quote: Singapore has seen tremendous prosperity and pretty good sustainable financing because they own 80% of the land and are able to do variable rate leases

insights.claim: Community land-trust models can create virtuous reinvestment loops: Scottish islands like Stornoway bought themselves via Scottish lottery proceeds routed to a national land bank, then built amenities (a golf course now part of the Scottish Open) because residents collectively capture the upside of improving the place.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Alternative land-value-capture model (collective ownership) that aligns local incentives to invest in place quality.

insights.claim: Many state constitutions, including Washington's, have a 'uniformity clause' requiring all real property be taxed as one class at one rate, which legally prohibits a straightforward split-rate land value tax.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: The legal barrier that forces reformers into indirect designs; relevant to which states can adopt LVT and how.
insights.quote: the state constitution has what's called a uniformity clause, which basically says that taxes on property must be uniform and that all real estate is one class

insights.claim: The workaround to the uniformity clause is a 'universal building exemption': the legislature grants cities authority to exempt a fixed percentage (e.g., 50%) of every building's value from taxation, which shrinks the taxable base, forces the uniform rate up, and effectively lands most of the tax on land — a revenue-neutral backdoor land value tax.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: The specific legal mechanism reformers are pushing; how LVT gets implemented where split-rate is unconstitutional.
insights.quote: What if we just exempted all buildings from taxation? the only thing left to tax is land.

insights.claim: Washington caps total property taxes at 1% of full market value across all jurisdictions combined and limits annual revenue growth to 1% per year — a district-wide cap unlike California's parcel-level Prop 13, but one that mechanically starves the fastest-growing cities of revenue.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Constrains how far cities can dial LVT and caps upside from growth; a structural fiscal headwind for high-growth WA cities.
insights.quote: the constitution caps the total amount of that across all of the different... you can't go over 1%. And additionally... It can only raise its total revenue collected by 1% every year.

insights.claim: Voter-approved levies in Washington are exempt from the 1% rate cap and the 1% annual-growth cap, giving cities like Seattle and Spokane a democratic route around the property-tax limits — though persuading voters to raise their own taxes is difficult.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Identifies the practical escape valve for WA municipal revenue and LVT implementation despite the caps.
insights.quote: the city can vote for levies... and when they vote for levies those voted levies do not apply to either of those restrictions

insights.claim: California's Prop 13, being parcel-level, produces extreme inequities where two adjacent $5 million homes can pay wildly different taxes ($2,000 vs. $100,000) based on how long they've been owned — a distinct and worse distortion than Washington's district-wide caps.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Highlights Prop 13's lock-in distortion as separate from LVT debate; relevant to CA housing-market immobility.
insights.quote: you'll have two properties sitting next to each other. One will be taxed at $2,000, one will be taxed at $100,000. and you know they're one they're both $5 million homes

insights.claim: Spokane over-drew its urban growth boundary early after Washington's Growth Management Act, leaving it with a large boundary, a sprawl and infrastructure/traffic problem, and a not-affluent tax base — and despite upzoning, transit investment, streetscaping, and a form-based code, it still has striking amounts of underdeveloped land and vacant lots in the city center.
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: Case study that the full zoning-reform toolkit can be exhausted without producing infill, motivating tax reform.
insights.quote: we have gone as far as we can in Spokane with upzoning and incentives

insights.claim: In Center for Land Economics' modeled Spokane proposal (a base exemption plus a percentage building exemption), the median single-family homeowner's property tax burden falls about 4%, and the large majority of single-family homes stay within 10% of their current bill.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts the 'homeowners get hammered' fear; the median owner benefits, which is the political linchpin.
insights.quote: the median single family homeowner is going to decrease 4%... this large majority of single family homes will stay within 10% of their current bill

insights.claim: Spokane's modeled shift moves the tax burden onto vacant and surface parking lots in the downtown corridor and off multifamily developments and townhouses, with multifamily seeing a median ~10% tax decrease — a pattern Center for Land Economics says holds across most cities it models.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Quantifies winners (multifamily, ~-10%) and losers (idle land) — the reprice that shifts development economics.
insights.quote: your multif family is typically a median 10% decrease

insights.claim: A key doubt about efficacy: under the modeled Spokane split the median vacant parcel's tax bill rises only about $560 a year, while nearby land values could be appreciating tens of thousands of dollars a year from public investment, raising the question of whether the tax is large enough to actually change speculator behavior.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: The bear case on the reform's real-world bite — a modest carrying-cost bump may not overcome large expected land appreciation.
insights.quote: the median tax bill of a vacant parcel will rise by something like $560 a year. But these... land values could be appreciating by tens of thousands of dollars a year

insights.claim: Spokane plans roughly a quarter-billion dollars of public investment in bus rapid transit, stations, and streetscaping along its Division corridor, which is currently full of vacant lots and cinder-block empty buildings — and officials fear that investment will fail to spur private development without a land tax to counter speculative holding.
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: Concrete example of public capital at risk of being captured as private land windfall absent value-capture policy.
insights.quote: It's going to be a quarter of a billion dollars of public investment and then see nothing grow out of that.

insights.claim: Spokane has no single-family-only zoning — a fourplex is already allowed anywhere — so a land value tax would not change what can be built and would not itself induce apartments in single-family neighborhoods, countering the standard homeowner fear.
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: Decouples the tax from zoning fears; the messaging strategy to defuse homeowner opposition.
insights.quote: it doesn't affect the zoning at all. So, if you're not zoned for multif family, you're not going to see multif family just because of a land value tax

insights.claim: Spokane cannot reform its own taxes without state authority and is seeking either a Spokane-specific pilot or permissive statewide legislation, but the bill stalled this session because drafting requires Department of Revenue cooperation that has not materialized and legislators 'get spooked when the details come out.'
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: Timeline/probability read: implementation is gated on state-level buy-in and bureaucratic drafting capacity, not yet resolved.
insights.quote: they get the idea and the concept and then they get spooked when the details come out

insights.claim: Washington's state assessors association unanimously opposed Spokane's proposal and the county assessor called it 'crazy' on grounds it would require running two separate tax rolls, making assessor resistance a primary practical barrier to adoption.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Administrative gatekeepers are a real veto point; investors tracking adoption should watch assessor buy-in as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: your county assessor called this proposal crazy... The state assessors association were unanimous in opposing this

insights.claim: Advocates counter that no new assessment method is needed: assessors already value land and buildings separately (Spokane does this well), already administer building-improvement exemptions like the multifamily tax exemption, and would only need to add one more exemption — potentially just a programming/algorithm change, not new staff.
insights.speaker: Kitty Klitsky
insights.so_what: If correct, the administrative objection is soft and adoption friction is lower than opponents claim.
insights.quote: This could be a matter of just programming and not necessarily having to hire new staff.

insights.claim: Assessment jurisdictions are chronically understaffed relative to the task, and Pennsylvania avoided the workload by simply not reassessing, whereas Washington reassesses annually — meaning reassessment cadence, not the LVT itself, drives the political shocks.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: States with frequent reassessment (like WA) may implement LVT more durably than those with stale rolls.

insights.claim: Legislative momentum for enabling split-rate land value taxes has jumped from roughly a couple of states considering it three to four years ago to about 10-12 states with introduced legislation now, with two states passing enabling bills in 2026 — the first such passages in roughly two decades.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: A fast-accelerating policy trend; a leading signal that land-carrying-cost economics could shift in more US cities.
insights.quote: three years ago we're looking at a couple of of states. Now we're looking at 1210 with introduced legislation

insights.claim: In 2026 Virginia (via a fairly bipartisan bill) enabled four additional cities including Newport News and Charlottesville to adopt split-rate taxes, and Kentucky enabled Louisville, showing concrete legislative wins.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Names the specific jurisdictions where LVT could newly reshape development incentives.
insights.quote: Virginia enabled four additional cities. Uh Newport News and Charlottesville are like the sort of main ones. and then Kentucky enabled Louisville

insights.claim: Land value taxation is strikingly cross-partisan: Kentucky's Republican legislature passed the split-rate provision as part of a larger governance overhaul (over a Democratic governor's veto), pitching it as a pro-development strategy for urban areas, while Democrats also supported the provision even when opposing the broader bill.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Bipartisan appeal raises the policy's odds of durable, wider adoption versus typical single-party reforms.
insights.quote: a Democrat stands up and like, I hate this bill, but I love the land value tax provision

insights.claim: The conservative case for land value taxes is that they are pro-development and efficient — former Senator Pat Toomey, as Allentown's city administrator, wrote to nearby cities urging them to adopt split-rate land value taxes as 'the best tax.'
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Evidence the right can champion LVT on growth/efficiency grounds, broadening the adoption coalition.
insights.quote: You guys should do split rate land value taxes. This is the best tax

insights.claim: Land speculators are a uniquely defenseless political target — almost no one outside the speculators themselves defends them — which advocates see as a structural advantage for taxing land over other bases.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Suggests the political path of least resistance for raising revenue points toward land — a tailwind for the policy.
insights.quote: almost nobody really loves land speculators

insights.claim: Interest is reaching mainstream politics: Seattle mayor Katie Wilson recently expressed interest in pursuing a land value tax, and cities that previously abandoned the policy (Pittsburgh, where the Post-Gazette's editors called for reviving it, and Vancouver) are now seeing revival efforts.
insights.speaker: Greg Miller
insights.so_what: Signals the idea is moving from niche Georgist circles into big-city executive agendas — watch these cities for pilots.
